one_line_takeaway: Across 1,033 Solidity contracts generated by GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.5, and Claude Sonnet-4.5 via GitHub Copilot, Slither found at least one low/medium/high vulnerability in 47-75%+ of contracts depending on model (Sonnet-4.5 worst by rate and low/medium volume, Gemini-2.5 worst by high-severity count), with vulnerability count rising ~15% per extra 100 lines of code -- evidence that LLM-generated smart contracts should be treated as untrusted code requiring mandatory security review before deployment.
key_finding: Vulnerability spawn rate by model: GPT-4.1 47.4% of generated contracts vulnerable, Gemini-2.5 53.2%, Sonnet-4.5 over 75% (paper states 'three out of four'). LoC statistics: median 75 LoC per contract overall (Q1=40, Q3=182, max=595); by model, Sonnet-4.5 generated far longer contracts (mean 222.18 LoC, total 69,987 LoC) than Gemini-2.5 (mean 58.63 LoC, total 21,343) or GPT-4.1 (mean 33.13 LoC, total 11,729). The Poisson GLM found LoC significantly predicts vulnerability count (coefficient 0.0014634, z=6.793, p=1.1e-11), interpreted as roughly a 15% increase in vulnerability count per additional 100 LoC. Total vulnerability counts by type and model (Table V): GPT-4.1=232, Gemini-2.5=294, Sonnet-4.5=564, with Sonnet-4.5 dominating in 'Others' (235), Re-entrancy (138), Access Control (70) and Logic Errors (67); Gemini-2.5 was the only model whose output contained Unchecked External Call findings (18); GPT-4.1 and Gemini-2.5 had comparatively more Lack-of-Input-Validation and Denial-of-Service findings than Sonnet-4.5. By severity (Table VI): Low counts were GPT-4.1=174, Gemini-2.5=236, Sonnet-4.5=458; Medium were GPT-4.1=34, Gemini-2.5=17, Sonnet-4.5=90; High were GPT-4.1=24, Gemini-2.5=41, Sonnet-4.5=16 -- i.e., Sonnet-4.5 produced the most contracts overall and the most low/medium findings but the fewest high-severity findings, while Gemini-2.5 produced the most high-severity findings (41) despite a lower overall vulnerability count. DeFi-domain prompts produced the most vulnerabilities of any functional category; Governance & Voting the fewest, though the authors caution this partly reflects the uneven number of prompts per category rather than a pure domain effect.
core_contribution: An empirical security measurement study of Solidity smart contracts generated by three commercial LLM coding agents (GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.5, Claude Sonnet-4.5, all accessed via the GitHub Copilot VS Code plugin). The authors build a five-stage pipeline: (1) collect 50 vulnerable Solidity contracts from SmartBugs, OWASP Smart Contract Top 10, and the SWC Registry; (2) use GPT-4.1 to extract high-level functional-intent prompts from 52 sampled contracts; (3) use GPT-5-mini to mutate/paraphrase these into 34 'junior-developer-style' prompts across 7 functional domains (DeFi, token management, governance, communication, identity, trading, utility); (4) feed each of the 34 prompts to each of the 3 models for 10 generation iterations (one-shot, via Copilot chat), yielding 1,033 generated Solidity contracts; (5) run every generated contract through the Slither static analyzer and aggregate/severity-tag the findings in R/Python/PowerShell scripts. This is presented as the first systematic study of security flaws specifically arising from LLM-generated (as opposed to human-written or model-audited) smart contracts.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model (Section IV): an inexperienced/junior developer submits a natural-language specification to a commercial LLM coding assistant (e.g., GitHub Copilot backed by GPT-4.1/Gemini-2.5/Sonnet-4.5) via a chat interface, reviews the returned code only for syntactic/functional correctness (not security), and deploys it. Because deployed contracts are immutable, any residual vulnerability becomes permanently exploitable. Adversaries are assumed to be able to read the fully public, deployed bytecode/source and to use both automated scanners (Slither, MythX) and manual/dynamic analysis to find and exploit flaws; the paper also notes the same LLMs could in principle be used by attackers to find vulnerabilities in public contracts, though it does not itself test this attacker-side use case. Note this is a code-generation-safety/software-security threat model over blockchain artifacts, not a jailbreak or prompt-injection threat model against the LLMs themselves.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) data-generation was manual (feeding prompts to the Copilot chat UI one at a time) to keep costs low, limiting dataset scale and reproducibility automation; (2) Slither's classification taxonomy was adopted 'for convenience' and does not cover flash-loan vulnerabilities from the OWASP taxonomy; (3) Slither is a static, signature-based analyzer and cannot detect complex market-based/economic logic vulnerabilities; (4) each generated project consisted of only 1-2 Solidity files, unlike realistic multi-file production projects; (5) the original seed-dataset vulnerability labels (SmartBugs/OWASP/SWC, which use differing and in the SWC case unmaintained-since-2020 taxonomies) do not map cleanly onto the vulnerability categories used in the detection/analysis phase, so Table I (seed distribution) is not directly comparable to Tables V/VI (detected distribution). The paper does not test LLM-based auditing/self-review as a mitigation empirically (only discusses it qualitatively), and does not evaluate domain-specific/blockchain-fine-tuned models, only general-purpose ones.

datasets_used: SmartBugs dataset (seed vulnerable contracts from Etherscan/public sources)
Self-constructed prompt dataset: 52 feature-extraction prompts -> 34 final mutated prompts across 7 functional domains
Self-constructed generated-contract corpus: 1,033 Solidity contracts (3 models x 34 prompts x 10 iterations)
quantitative_results: Vulnerability spawn rate: GPT-4.1 47.4%, Gemini-2.5 53.2%, Sonnet-4.5 >75%
Mean LoC per contract by model: GPT-4.1 33.13, Gemini-2.5 58.63, Sonnet-4.5 222.18
Total vulnerability findings by model: GPT-4.1 232, Gemini-2.5 294, Sonnet-4.5 564
Poisson GLM: LoC coefficient 0.0014634 (SE 0.0002154, z=6.793, p=1.1e-11) -> ~15% more vulnerabilities per +100 LoC
Overall LoC distribution: min 16, Q1 40, median 75, mean 128.5, Q3 182, max 595
Experiment cost: $39 (one month GitHub Copilot Pro Plus, 680 paid premium requests)
baselines_compared: No prior LLM-generated-smart-contract security baseline exists per the authors' literature review; comparison is model-vs-model within this study, not against a published prior system
